title: Code Review Culture at FAANG
description: Code review at FAANG is not a gate — it is a conversation, a teaching tool, and the primary mechanism for maintaining code quality at scale. Unlike SAP's transport-based review system (where code moves through a hierarchy of approvals), FAANG code review is peer-based, asynchronous, and every engineer participates. This guide covers the norms, expectations, and social dynamics of FAANG code review, how to write comments that teach, how to handle critical reviews, and the specific adjustments SAP engineers need to make.

sections.title: Why Code Review at FAANG is Different from SAP

description.value:
In SAP environments, code review is typically a transport-approval process. You move your code through a chain: developer → quality manager → transport administrator → production. Each step is a gate that checks whether the code compiles, whether the transport conflicts with others, whether the functional spec was followed. The review is about process compliance, not code quality.

FAANG code review fundamentals: (1) Every change is reviewed by at least one peer before it is merged. No exceptions — not for hotfixes, not for the CEO's pet project, not for Sunday 2 AM emergencies. (2) The reviewer's job is not to approve or reject — it is to improve the change and teach the author. A review that ends with 'LGTM' (Looks Good To Me) but no comments is a missed opportunity. (3) The author owns the change from first commit to production. The reviewer provides feedback, but the author decides what to incorporate. The reviewer does not 'sign off' — they 'approve' or 'request changes.' (4) Review velocity is a team metric. A change that sits in review for 3 days is a failure of the review system, not a failure of the author. (5) Review culture is explicit — teams and companies have written review guidelines, expectations for turnaround time, and escalation paths for stuck reviews.

Key differences from SAP: (1) SAP review is hierarchical (manager/architect approves). FAANG review is peer-based — your reviewer could be a junior engineer or a Staff+ engineer. (2) SAP review focuses on process compliance (right transport, right system). FAANG review focuses on code quality, design, and test coverage. (3) SAP review is synchronous (you sit with the reviewer or wait for a meeting). FAANG review is async — you post the diff, the reviewer comments when they have time. (4) SAP rejects are final ('this transport is rejected — fix and resubmit'). FAANG 'request changes' is a starting point for discussion — you can discuss, disagree, and find a middle ground. (5) SAP review is done by a designated quality manager. FAANG review is done by anyone — and senior engineers are expected to review more, not less.

Why SAP engineers struggle: (1) The lack of hierarchy is disorienting. A junior engineer can request changes to your Staff+ code. In SAP, that would be unusual. At FAANG, it is expected — the change is judged on its merits, not the author's level. (2) The async nature feels slow. At SAP, you might sit with the reviewer for 30 minutes and resolve everything. At FAANG, a review can take 2-3 rounds over 24-48 hours. This feels inefficient but produces higher quality — each round gives you time to think, not just react. (3) The feedback can feel personal. In SAP, transport rejections are mechanical. At FAANG, reviewers comment on your logic, your style, your approach. Learning to separate 'feedback on the change' from 'feedback on you' is essential.

sections.codeBlock:
# SAP Transport Review vs FAANG Code Review

| Dimension | SAP Transport Review | FAANG Code Review |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Who reviews | Quality Manager / Transport Admin | Any peer engineer |
| What is reviewed | Transport consistency, process compliance | Code quality, design, tests, edge cases |
| Review mode | Synchronous (meetings, phone calls) | Asynchronous (comments on diff) |
| Authority | Hierarchical (manager can override) | Meritocratic (best argument wins) |
| Feedback style | 'Rejected — fix transport order' | 'Consider using Optional.ofNullable here' |
| Learning outcome | Process compliance | Technical growth through discussion |
| Velocity norm | 1-2 days (queue dependent) | <24 hours for first review |
| Hotfix process | Emergency transport, post-approval | Same process, expedited review |
| Tool | Solman / Charm / CTS | Phabricator / Critique / ReviewBoard |
| Stakes | Transport blocks go-live | Merge blocks deploy |

sections.title: Review Expectations by Level

description.value:
Code review is not the same for every engineer. Your level determines what you are expected to review, how deeply, and what your reviews should teach. At the Staff+ level, code review is a leadership tool — you are not just reviewing code, you are setting technical standards and growing the team's skills.

Junior/New graduate (L3/E3/L59): Expected to review 1-2 changes per week. Focus on reading code and understanding patterns. Comments should be questions and observations, not directives. 'Why did you choose this approach?' is better than 'Use a different approach.' Learning to review is as important as learning to code.

Mid-level (L4/E4/L61): Expected to review 3-5 changes per week. Focus on correctness, test coverage, and edge cases. Should catch bugs, missing tests, and error handling gaps. Reviews should include positive reinforcement ('Nice use of the builder pattern here') alongside constructive feedback.

Senior (L5/E5/L63): Expected to review 5-8 changes per week. Focus on design decisions, performance implications, and maintainability. Should review not just their own team's code but also cross-team changes that affect their systems. Reviews should teach the 'why' behind the feedback, not just the 'what.'

Staff+ (L6/E6/L64+): Expected to review 5-10 changes per week, but more importantly, to set the review standards for the team. Staff+ engineers should: (1) Review the hardest changes — concurrency issues, security implications, architectural decisions. (2) Mentor through reviews — use reviews as a teaching tool, not just a quality gate. (3) Establish team conventions — define what a 'good' review looks like, what should be in every review checklist, and how to handle disagreements. (4) Review reviewers — if a mid-level engineer gives poor feedback, the Staff+ engineer should coach them privately. (5) Keep review queues healthy — if reviews are stuck, the Staff+ engineer should be the backup reviewer or escalate.

Review ratio expectation: A healthy team has a 2:1 or 3:1 review-to-author ratio. For every change you author, you should review 2-3 changes from others. At the Staff+ level, this ratio should be higher (4-5 reviews per authored change) because your reviews have higher teaching value. If you are only authoring changes and not reviewing others', you are not contributing to the team's velocity — you are a net consumer of review capacity.

sections.codeBlock:
# Review Expectations by Level

def expected_reviews(level):
    """Expected weekly review count by level."""
    expectations = {
        'L3/Junior': {'min': 1, 'max': 2, 'focus': 'understanding patterns'},
        'L4/Mid':    {'min': 3, 'max': 5, 'focus': 'correctness, tests'},
        'L5/Senior': {'min': 5, 'max': 8, 'focus': 'design, performance'},
        'L6/Staff':  {'min': 5, 'max': 10, 'focus': 'standards, mentorship'},
        'L7/Principal': {'min': 3, 'max': 6, 'focus': 'architectural, cross-team'},
    }
    return expectations.get(level, {'min': 2, 'max': 4, 'focus': 'general'})

# Review ratio: for each change you author, review N changes from others
REVIEW_RATIO_BY_LEVEL = {
    'L3': 1,   # 1 review per authored change
    'L4': 2,   # 2:1 review-to-author ratio
    'L5': 3,   # 3:1
    'L6': 4,   # 4:1
    'L7': 5,   # 5:1
}

# Team health metric: average review turnaround time
# Target: <24 hours for first review (P50), <48 hours (P95)
# If P95 > 72h, the team has a review bottleneck

def review_queue_health(reviews):
    pending_24h = sum(1 for r in reviews if r.age_hours > 24)
    pending_48h = sum(1 for r in reviews if r.age_hours > 48)
    pending_72h = sum(1 for r in reviews if r.age_hours > 72)
    return {
        'pending_24h': pending_24h,
        'pending_48h': pending_48h,
        'pending_72h': pending_72h,
        'healthy': pending_72h == 0
    }

decisionTree.prompt: What review depth is appropriate for this change?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: What type of change is this?

branches.condition: Trivial change — typo fix, comment update, minor refactor
branches.path: Surface-level review. Check for correctness and style. Approve quickly (within 4 hours) if nothing is wrong. The author should not wait 24 hours for a typo fix. If the same typo keeps happening, address the systemic issue (add a linter rule) rather than writing a detailed review.

branches.condition: Medium change — new feature, non-trivial bug fix, moderate refactor
branches.path: Standard review depth. Check: correctness, test coverage, edge cases, error handling, performance implications. Expect 1-3 review rounds. Provide specific, actionable feedback. Include positive observations. Target first review within 12 hours. This is the most common review type — most of your reviews will be at this depth.

branches.condition: Major architectural change — new service, significant refactor, database migration
branches.path: Deep review. Schedule a 30-minute synchronous review session after the initial async review. The async review catches surface issues; the sync review discusses tradeoffs the author may not have considered. Review: design decisions, failure modes, rollback plan, monitoring, security, data consistency. Expect 3-5 review rounds over 2-5 days. This review should be treated as a mini design review.

branches.condition: Emergency hotfix — production issue that needs immediate fix
branches.path: Lightweight review. Focus on: does this fix the immediate issue? Are there any obvious side effects? Will this break anything else? Approve within 30 minutes if the fix is correct. The author should follow up with a postmortem and a more thorough review of the permanent fix. Do not use emergency as an excuse to skip review entirely — even a 2-minute scan catches most catastrophic mistakes.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The most common review mistake at the Staff+ level is reviewing everything at the same depth. A typo fix does not need the same scrutiny as a database migration. Match your review depth to the risk level of the change. Your review queue will clear faster, and your reviews of high-risk changes will be more thorough because you are not exhausted from reviewing trivial changes.

sections.title: How to Write Review Comments That Teach

description.value:
The difference between a good reviewer and a great reviewer is not how many bugs they catch — it is whether the author learns something from the review. A review comment that teaches prevents the same mistake from being made again. A review comment that just states the problem fixes the code once but not the engineer.

Levels of review comments (from least to most useful): (1) Statement of problem — 'This variable name is confusing.' (Least useful. The author knows the variable is named poorly. What do you want them to call it?) (2) Statement of preference — 'I would use a different name here.' (Slightly better. But why? What principle guides your preference?) (3) Principle + alternative — 'Variable names should reflect the domain, not the implementation. Use 'invoiceStatus' instead of 'statusFlag' — this makes the intent clear without reading the context.' (Useful. Teaches the principle and shows the alternative.) (4) Principle + question — 'We prefer domain-aligned naming in this codebase. What makes 'invoiceStatus' a better name than 'statusFlag' here?' (Most useful. Teaches the principle and makes the author think. The next time they name a variable, they will apply the principle themselves.)

The 'why' pattern: Every review comment should include the 'why' behind the feedback. 'This approach won't scale because it loads all records into memory. The payment service processes 10K events/sec. Instead, use pagination or streaming.' Without the 'why,' the author learns to follow instructions. With the 'why,' the author learns to think about scalability.

Positive reinforcement pattern: Reviews should include positive comments, not just negative ones. 'Great use of the strategy pattern here — clean separation of concerns.' 'The test coverage for edge cases in this change is excellent.' Positive comments reinforce good patterns and build trust. If all your reviews are negative, the author will dread seeing your name as a reviewer.

The comment-to-approve ratio: A healthy review has 3-10 comments for a medium change. Zero comments (just 'LGTM') is a missed opportunity — there is always something to teach or learn. 20+ comments means the change may benefit from a design discussion before the code review.

sections.codeBlock:
# Bad review comments
"This is wrong."                         # No explanation
"Fix this variable name."                 # No principle, no suggestion
"This will be slow."                      # No evidence, no alternative
"You should use a factory pattern."       # Pattern cargo-culting

# Good review comments
"Using a magic number '86400' makes this
hard to maintain. Define a constant
SECONDS_IN_DAY = 86400 with a comment
explaining why this specific value is used.
(This also makes it searchable.)"

"This API call is inside a loop — network
requests should be batched. At 10K items,
this will make 10K HTTP calls instead of ~10.
Use the batch endpoint instead, which accepts
up to 1000 items per request."

"Great job covering the success path in tests.
For robustness, consider adding a test for the
error case: what happens when the API returns
a 503? The current code will crash with a
NullPointerException. Adding an error handler
here would prevent a production incident."

# Teaching comment pattern
"[Observation] This method is 120 lines long.
[Principle] Methods should do one thing and
be testable independently. [Suggestion] Could
you extract lines 40-80 into a separate method
called 'validateOrderStatus'? This makes the
main method readable at a glance and allows
unit testing the validation logic separately."

# Positive feedback pattern
"The way you handled the error case here
(graceful degradation with a fallback value)
is exactly right. We should do this more
often across the service."

sections.title: How to Handle Getting Difficult Reviews

description.value:
Every engineer — Staff+ included — receives review comments that feel harsh, pedantic, or wrong. How you handle these reviews defines your professional reputation more than how you write them. At FAANG, the ability to receive critical feedback without defensiveness is a core competency, especially at the Staff+ level where your code is held to the highest standard.

Rule 1: Separate intent from impact. The reviewer's intent is almost always to improve the code, not to criticize you. Even if the comment feels personal ('This is a terrible approach'), assume positive intent: 'They care about the codebase enough to push back.' If the comment is genuinely rude, address the rudeness separately (talk to them 1:1) but still evaluate the technical merit of their feedback.

Rule 2: Default to curiosity, not defense. When you disagree with a comment, ask 'What am I missing?' instead of 'You're wrong.' 'I chose this approach because [reasoning]. Can you help me understand why you prefer the alternative?' This turns a potential argument into a learning conversation. You might learn something, and the reviewer might learn from your reasoning.

Rule 3: You don't have to accept every comment. Reviewers make mistakes too. If you disagree after considering their reasoning, explain your position and push back respectfully. 'I considered that approach, but rejected it because [reason]. Here is why I think my approach is better for this specific case. Would you be open to discussing further?' A well-reasoned pushback is respected — it shows you have thought deeply about the design.

Rule 4: Separate style from substance. If the reviewer suggests a different way to format code, name a variable, or structure a function — and you don't have a strong preference — just accept the suggestion. Style battles waste time. Say 'Good catch, will fix.' Save your pushback energy for substantive disagreements about logic, performance, or design.

Rule 5: The three-review-round rule. If a review goes beyond 3 rounds on a medium-sized change, it is a failure of the process, not the people. Either the change is larger than it should be (split it up), or the reviewer and author are not aligned on the approach (schedule a sync to resolve). Do not let a review stretch to 7 rounds of back-and-forth comments.

SAP-specific challenge: SAP engineers are not used to having their code challenged by peers. In SAP, the transport review is mechanical — it checks process compliance, not design quality. The first time a junior engineer tells an SAP senior architect 'Your approach has a thread-safety issue,' it can feel insulting. It is not. It is FAANG culture — the code speaks, not the title. Learn to hear the feedback, not the level of the person giving it.

sections.codeBlock:
# How to Respond to Review Comments

## When you agree
"Good catch, will fix." ✓
"Thanks for catching that — fixed." ✓
"Great point about the edge case. Added a test." ✓

## When you disagree but want to understand
"I chose this approach because [reason].
What am I missing?" ✓

"I considered that alternative. My concern
was [reason]. Can we discuss the tradeoffs?" ✓

## When you disagree and can defend your position
"I agree with your concern about X, but
I believe the tradeoff is worth it because
[reason]. Here is my analysis of both
approaches: [link to doc]. Happy to discuss
more in person." ✓

## When it's a style preference
"Good point, I'll update to match the
convention." ✓ (say this even if you prefer your way)

## What NOT to say
"That's not how we did it at SAP." ✗
"I'm L6, I think I know what I'm doing." ✗
"This change is too small to worry about." ✗
"I don't have time to fix this." ✗
"The code works fine as-is." ✗

adversarialResponse.challenge: "My reviewer is being pedantic. They left 15 comments on a simple bug fix — including a debate about variable naming conventions. This feels like a waste of time. Can I just merge without their approval?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Yes, you can override their review if you're Staff+. You outrank them." (introduces hierarchy into peer review — poisons the culture)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Two things: (1) Separately — were 15 comments on a simple bug fix? If yes, that reviewer may need feedback about proportionality. Review depth should match change risk. But first, check: are any of the 15 comments substantive? One of them might reveal a real issue. (2) Your options: Option A — fix the substantive comments (there are probably only 2-3 real issues) and reply to the style comments with 'Noted, will keep in mind for future changes.' Most reviewers will approve after you've addressed the substantive ones. Option B — if the reviewer is blocking on style preferences that are not team conventions, escalate to the team lead: 'We disagree on naming conventions. Can we establish a team standard?' Option C — never merge without approval on the first round. It sets a precedent that reviews are optional. If the review is truly stuck after 3 rounds, schedule a 15-minute sync to resolve face-to-face. A quick conversation resolves what 7 rounds of async comments cannot."
adversarialResponse.pattern: de-escalate-then-escalate — separate substantive from style, address substantive comments, resolve style with conventions, escalate only if blocked after good-faith effort. Never bypass the review process — you are Staff+ because you set the example, not because you have special privileges.

sections.title: Review Velocity and Queue Management

description.value:
A review that sits for 3 days costs more than just delayed feature delivery. It costs context — the author has to re-load the change in their head, the team loses momentum, and the review ends up being lower quality because the reviewer has to re-read the entire diff. Review velocity is a first-class metric at FAANG, and Staff+ engineers are expected to keep the queue moving.

Target turnaround times: (1) First review: <12 hours for standard changes, <4 hours for urgent changes, <30 minutes for P0/hotfixes. (2) Follow-up review: <4 hours (author should not wait longer for a re-review than for the first review). (3) Review queue size: 0-3 pending reviews is healthy. 5+ is a bottleneck — stop writing code and clear reviews. (4) If you can't review within 24 hours, unassign yourself and find a replacement reviewer. Unassigned reviews are worse than slow reviews — at least with a slow review, the author knows who is blocking them.

Queue management strategies for Staff+: (1) Review at the start of your day — do not open your IDE until your review queue is at 0-2. Reviews are the highest-leverage use of your time because they unblock the entire team. (2) Use the 'review in 24 hours' rule — if a review is pending for 24 hours without a comment from the assigned reviewer, auto-escalate to the Staff+ engineer as a backup. (3) Batch reviews — set 2-3 'review blocks' per day (30 minutes each) instead of responding to review requests in real-time throughout the day. This prevents constant context switching. (4) Delegate — if you have 5+ pending reviews, delegate 2-3 to senior engineers on the team who need review practice. 'I have capacity issues today. Can you take this review? I'll do a quick pass after you.'

The author's responsibility: Review velocity is not just the reviewer's responsibility. Authors can help: (1) Keep changes small — a change that touches 50 files is harder to review than 5 changes that each touch 10 files. Break large changes into reviewable chunks. (2) Write a good change description — explain what the change does, why it is needed, what testing was done, and what the reviewer should focus on. A good description reduces review time by 30-50%. (3) Tag the right reviewers — do not tag 5 people when 1 is sufficient. Tagging extra reviewers slows everyone down. (4) Respond to comments quickly — if the reviewer comes back within 4 hours, you respond within 4 hours. Review velocity is a two-way street.

sections.codeBlock:
# Change Description Template

## Title (50 chars max)
feat(payments): Add retry logic for failed card charges

## Description
**Why:** The payment provider occasionally returns
503 errors during peak hours (1-2% of requests).
Currently, these fail immediately, requiring the
user to retry manually. This adds retry logic with
exponential backoff for transient failures.

**What changed:**
- Added retry interceptor to PaymentClient (3 retries)
- Added exponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s
- Added metrics for retry attempts and success rates

**Testing:**
- Unit tests: retry_success, retry_exhausted, non_retryable_error
- Integration test: simulated 503 from test provider
- Manual: tested with 10 retry scenarios locally

**Focus areas for review:**
- The backoff calculation in PaymentClient.java:45
- Is 3 retries with max 7s total delay appropriate?
- Error handling for non-retryable errors

**Risks:**
- Increased latency for payments that eventually fail
  (max 7s delay before failure vs instant failure)
- No changes to the payment API contract

**Rollback plan:** Revert this change and re-deploy

sections.title: The Social Dynamics of Code Review

description.value:
Code review is a social interaction, not a technical process. Engineers who understand the social dynamics get their changes approved faster, build stronger relationships with their teammates, and become more influential in the organization. Engineers who treat code review as purely technical alienate their peers and slow the team down.

The reviewer-author power dynamic: Even though FAANG review is peer-based, there is always a perceived power imbalance. A Staff+ engineer reviewing a junior engineer's code creates deference — the junior may accept comments they disagree with because 'the Staff+ engineer must know better.' As the Staff+ reviewer, explicitly invite pushback: 'This is my opinion, not a requirement. If you have a compelling reason for your approach, I'm open to hearing it.' As the junior author, learn to push back respectfully: 'I considered that approach but chose this one because [reason]. Does that change your recommendation?'

The 'LGTM with caveat' trap: Some reviewers say 'LGTM' (Looks Good To Me) but add 'but I didn't look at the test file' or 'but I didn't review the migration script.' This is dangerous — the author thinks they have approval, but there are un-reviewed parts of the change. If you approve a change, you approve the entire change. If you cannot review the entire change, leave clear comments about what you did not review and suggest an additional reviewer for the uncovered areas.

The drive-by comment: A non-assigned reviewer leaves a comment on a change they were not tagged on. This can be helpful ('I noticed a security issue here') or disruptive ('You should use a different framework'). As the author, welcome helpful drive-by comments and politely defer disruptive ones. 'Thanks for the suggestion. For this change, I'm going to stay with the current approach because [reason]. Let's discuss the framework choice for the next major change.'

Review as relationship building: The best reviewers build relationships through reviews. They follow up after approving a significant change: 'Great work on that migration. Let me know if you want to discuss the performance improvements further.' They seek out authors whose code they respect and ask them to review their own changes. Reviews are one of the few times engineers interact 1:1 about their work — use this time to build trust and rapport, not just to fix bugs.

SAP-specific dynamic: In SAP, review is transactional — the transport is approved or rejected based on process rules. In FAANG, review is relational — how you give and receive feedback affects your standing on the team. Engineers who are generous reviewers (catching issues early, teaching through comments) become trusted partners. Engineers who are harsh or dismissive reviewers become people that authors avoid. Your reputation as a reviewer is your reputation as an engineer.

sections.codeBlock:
# Code Review Social Norms Cheat Sheet

## DO:
- Start with positive feedback
- Explain the 'why' behind every suggestion
- Admit when you're wrong
- Say 'thanks' when someone catches your mistake
- Approve quickly for small, correct changes
- Use emoji sparingly (one 👍 per review is fine)

## DON'T:
- Say 'LGTM' without reading the change
- Nitpick style without a team convention
- Make personal comments ('you always forget this')
- Leave drive-by comments on changes you're not assigned to
  (unless it's a security or correctness issue)
- Approve changes you haven't fully reviewed

## The 'Reviewer's Apology'
When you catch something in a review that you missed
in an earlier round: "I should have caught this in
round 1. Sorry for the late feedback." This shows
accountability and makes the author less frustrated.

## The 'Author's Thanks'
When someone catches a real bug in your review:
"Thanks — this would have caused an incident in
production. Really appreciate you catching it."
This reinforces the value of reviews and encourages
thorough review in the future.

sections.title: The SAP Engineer's Code Review Adjustment — A Practical Plan

description.value:
If you are coming from SAP, code review will be one of the biggest cultural adjustments. Here is a practical 90-day plan to go from 'SAP transport approval mindset' to 'FAANG peer review mindset.'

Days 1-30: Observation and learning. (1) Read 20-30 reviews from other engineers on your team before you submit your first change. Pay attention to: what kind of comments reviewers leave, how authors respond, how disagreements are resolved, and what gets approved vs sent back. (2) Ask a senior engineer: 'Can you walk me through your review process? What do you look for first? What do you consider a blocking issue vs a suggestion?' (3) Submit your first small change (bug fix, documentation update, test improvement) and observe the review process from the author side. Respond to every comment with 'Thanks, fixed' or 'Good question — here is my reasoning.' (4) After your first change is approved, ask the reviewer: 'What could I have done differently to make the review easier?'

Days 31-60: Start reviewing others. (1) Ask your manager or a senior engineer to assign you as a secondary reviewer on a change. 'I want to practice reviewing. Can I be added as a second reviewer on your next change?' (2) Focus your first reviews on test coverage and documentation — lower risk areas that still add value. (3) Use the comment templates from this guide: principle + alternative + question. (4) After you submit your review, ask the primary reviewer: 'Did I miss anything? What should I focus on next time?'

Days 61-90: Independent review. (1) Start reviewing changes as a primary reviewer. Start with changes from mid-level engineers (their reviews are less risky than Staff+ changes). (2) Build a personal review checklist: correctness, test coverage, error handling, edge cases, performance, security, style. (3) Track your review velocity: how long does it take you to complete a review? Are you faster than the team's target? (4) By day 90, you should be reviewing 3-5 changes per week and your reviews should be valued by the team.

The mindset shift summary: At SAP, a review is a gate that checks if your code follows the rules. At FAANG, a review is a conversation that improves the code and teaches the engineer. Your SAP experience with structured documentation and attention to detail is an advantage — you will read code more carefully than most native FAANG engineers. The adjustment is not about learning to review code — you already know how to do that. The adjustment is learning to review with empathy, to teach through comments, and to separate feedback on the code from feedback on the person.

sections.codeBlock:
# 90-Day Review Transition Plan

## Phase 1: Observe (Days 1-30)
- [ ] Read 20+ existing reviews on the team
- [ ] Identify team review patterns and conventions
- [ ] Submit 1-2 small changes, observe the process
- [ ] Ask for post-review feedback from the reviewer

## Phase 2: Review with supervision (Days 31-60)
- [ ] Secondary review on 3-5 changes (with a senior backup)
- [ ] Focus on: test coverage, documentation, error handling
- [ ] Use the 'principle + alternative + question' pattern
- [ ] Get feedback from primary reviewer after each review

## Phase 3: Review independently (Days 61-90)
- [ ] Primary reviewer for 5-10 changes
- [ ] Build personal review checklist
- [ ] Track review velocity (target: <12h for first review)
- [ ] Review ratio target: 3+ reviews per authored change

## Ongoing
- [ ] Maintain <24h review turnaround for assigned reviews
- [ ] Write review comments that teach (not just correct)
- [ ] Review hardest changes on the team (concurrency, security)
- [ ] Coach junior engineers on their review skills

items.label: I review at least as many changes as I author (2:1 ratio minimum)
My review comments explain the 'why' behind the feedback
I include positive observations, not just constructive criticism
My first review turnaround is under 12 hours for standard changes
I respond to reviewer comments on my changes within 24 hours
I can accept critical review feedback without being defensive
I push back respectfully when I disagree with a review comment
I match review depth to change risk (not reviewing everything at the same depth)
I keep my own changes small and reviewable (<300 lines when possible)
I write clear change descriptions that explain what, why, and what to focus on

details: FAANG Interview Tip: Code review questions appear in Staff+ behavioral interviews in subtle ways. 'Tell me about a time you gave critical feedback to a peer' or 'How do you handle disagreements about code quality?' are code review questions in disguise. Your answer should demonstrate: (1) You understand that code review is about teaching, not gatekeeping. (2) You can give feedback that is constructive, specific, and kind. (3) You can receive feedback without being defensive. (4) You can disagree productively and find consensus. An SAP story about 'I reviewed a transport that had a performance issue' can be framed as a FAANG code review story: 'I reviewed a change that loaded all records into memory instead of using pagination. I explained why pagination was necessary at our scale — the system processes 10K events per second — and offered to pair on the implementation. The author learned about streaming patterns, and the next change they authored already used pagination correctly.'
